methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: QuantumScape
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: LanzaTech
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Public), post-money $800M+

data.narrative:
Enapter is a public-stage Climate & Energy company founded by Sebastian-Justus Schmidt. The valuation exercise draws on a fragmented comp universe: one public peer (LanzaTech, ticker LNZA) and eight private competitors including QuantumScape, TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, and Helion. The broader Climate & Energy sector contains 19 public and private companies, but the narrow peer set suffers from material constraints.

Peer-median triangulation is not applicable here. The analysis identified only two same-stage peers within Climate & Energy, with zero adjacent-stage and zero similar-tier comparables. This falls below the minimum threshold of three datapoints required for reliable median-based valuation. Using the full 19-company sector median would conflate dissimilar business models, geographies, and development timelines, introducing unacceptable noise into the analysis.

Without sufficient narrow peer overlap or disclosed financial metrics across the private comparables, this valuation framework cannot credibly support a point estimate or range. Additional disclosure of revenue, EBITDA, or burn-rate metrics from the identified private peers—or identification of additional same-stage, same-sector public benchmarks—would be necessary to triangulate defensible multiples. The single public comp (LNZA) provides insufficient anchoring on its own. Valuation work should pause pending either richer data availability or a methodological pivot toward precedent transaction or DCF approaches with robust sensitivity analysis.
